title: Parallel Agents
subtitle: Run researcher and trend_researcher simultaneously. Add operator.add to safely merge parallel writes.

concept.heading: When two agents write to the same field
concept.body:
In v4 we have two creative agents: `social_creative` and `news_creative`. Both write to `creative_drafts`. They run in parallel.

Without a reducer, the second write silently overwrites the first. You lose all social drafts if news_creative finishes last. No error. Just missing content.

callouts.title: Silent overwrite — the hardest bug to find
callouts.body: social_creative returns {'creative_drafts': [A, B]}. news_creative returns {'creative_drafts': [C]}. LangGraph's default merge is last-write-wins. The planner sees only [C].

callouts.title: The fix: operator.add as a reducer
callouts.body: Annotated[list[dict], operator.add] tells LangGraph: when two nodes both return this field, ADD the lists together instead of replacing. Result: [A, B, C].

concept.heading: Annotated: attaching reducers to types
concept.body:
Python's `Annotated[T, metadata]` attaches extra information to a type annotation. LangGraph uses this pattern to attach reducer functions.

`Annotated[list[dict], operator.add]` means: this field is a list[dict], AND when merging concurrent writes, use operator.add (list concatenation).

endQuiz.stem: Two nodes run in parallel: A returns {'posts': ['tweet1']}, B returns {'posts': ['article1']}. Without operator.add, the merged state has:
options: Either ['tweet1'] OR ['article1'] — whichever finishes last overwrites
An error — duplicate field write is not allowed
endQuiz.explanation: LangGraph's default merge is last-write-wins for non-annotated fields. Whichever parallel branch finishes last overwrites the other — silently, with no error.

endQuiz.stem: True or False: Annotated[list[dict], operator.add] only works with lists.
endQuiz.explanation: operator.add works on any addable type. For integers it sums, for lists it concatenates. You can also pass custom reducer functions for other merge strategies.

lessons.title: Parallel Fan-Out with START

concept.heading: Two edges from START = parallel execution
concept.body:
Adding `add_edge(START, 'researcher')` AND `add_edge(START, 'trend_researcher')` tells LangGraph to launch both simultaneously.

LangGraph then automatically waits for BOTH to complete before running planner — this is **fan-in**. You get the synchronization for free.

callouts.title: Timing: sequential vs parallel
callouts.body: researcher takes ~15s. trend_researcher takes ~15s. Sequential = 30s. Parallel = 15s (max of both). At 2 runs/day for 30 days that is 15 minutes saved just on the research phase.

brokenCode.code:
from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
import operator
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END

class PipelineState(TypedDict):
    topic: str
    research: list[dict]
    trends: list[dict]
    content_briefs: list[dict]
    creative_drafts: ???   # ⚠ TODO 1:
    # Hint: social_creative AND news_creative BOTH write to this list.
    # They run simultaneously. Without a reducer, one silently overwrites the other.
    # What annotation makes two parallel writes MERGE instead of replace?
    platform_posts: list[dict]

builder = StateGraph(PipelineState)
builder.add_node("researcher",       researcher_node)
builder.add_node("trend_researcher", trend_researcher_node)
builder.add_node("planner",          planner_node)

builder.add_edge(START, "researcher")
???  # ⚠ TODO 2:
# Hint: trend_researcher is added as a node but nothing triggers it.
# researcher and trend_researcher should run at the SAME TIME.
# One line launches trend_researcher in parallel with researcher.
builder.add_edge("researcher",       "planner")
builder.add_edge("trend_researcher", "planner")
builder.add_edge("planner", END)
graph = builder.compile()

todos.marker:
???   # ⚠ TODO 1:
    # Hint: social_creative AND news_creative BOTH write to this list.
    # They run simultaneously. Without a reducer, one silently overwrites the other.
    # What annotation makes two parallel writes MERGE instead of replace?
    platform_posts: list[dict]
todos.whyItMatters: Without this, social_creative's drafts get silently overwritten by news_creative's (or vice versa). One parallel writer always wins and the other's work is lost. Annotated + operator.add makes LangGraph concatenate both lists.
todos.correctCode:
Annotated[list[dict], operator.add]   # parallel-safe: lists get merged
    platform_posts: Annotated[list[dict], operator.add]

todos.marker:
???  # ⚠ TODO 2:
# Hint: trend_researcher is added as a node but nothing triggers it.
# researcher and trend_researcher should run at the SAME TIME.
# One line launches trend_researcher in parallel with researcher.
builder.add_edge("researcher",       "planner")
todos.whyItMatters: Without this edge, trend_researcher is registered but never runs. The planner gets articles but zero trend data — no hashtags, no viral hooks, no Hinglish creative angle.

midQuiz.stem: Which annotation makes parallel writes to creative_drafts safe?
midQuiz.explanation: Annotated[T, reducer] is LangGraph's pattern. operator.add concatenates lists from concurrent writers instead of letting one overwrite the other.

midQuiz.stem: To launch trend_researcher in parallel with researcher: builder.add_edge(___, 'trend_researcher')
midQuiz.explanation: Two add_edge(START, ...) calls create parallel fan-out. LangGraph launches both simultaneously and waits for both before running planner (fan-in).

completeCode.code:
# pipeline.py — v4: Parallel agents + operator.add
import anthropic, json, asyncio
from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
import operator
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, START, END

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
TOOLS = [{"name": "web_search", "description": "Search for RE news",
           "input_schema": {"type": "object",
                            "properties": {"query": {"type": "string"}},
                            "required": ["query"]}}]

class PipelineState(TypedDict):
    topic: str
    research: list[dict]
    trends: list[dict]
    content_briefs: list[dict]
    creative_drafts: Annotated[list[dict], operator.add]   # parallel-safe
    platform_posts:  Annotated[list[dict], operator.add]   # parallel-safe

def mock_web_search(query):
    return [{"title": f"Result: {query}", "content": "Mumbai prices up 8% YoY"}]

def researcher_node(state: PipelineState) -> dict:
    messages = [{"role": "user", "content": f"Research: {state['topic']}"}]
    while True:
        resp = client.messages.create(model="claude-sonnet-4-6", max_tokens=1024,
                                      tools=TOOLS, messages=messages)
        if resp.stop_reason == "end_turn":
            return {"research": json.loads(resp.content[-1].text)}
        tool = next(b for b in resp.content if b.type == "tool_use")
        result = mock_web_search(tool.input["query"])
        messages += [{"role": "assistant", "content": resp.content},
                     {"role": "user", "content": [{"type": "tool_result",
                      "tool_use_id": tool.id, "content": json.dumps(result)}]}]

def trend_researcher_node(state: PipelineState) -> dict:
    return {"trends": [{"hashtag": "#MumbaiRealEstate", "platform": "twitter",
                        "volume": "15K", "context": "stamp duty cut discussion"}]}

def planner_node(state: PipelineState) -> dict:
    return {"content_briefs": [{"topic": state["topic"], "draft_type": "social",
                                 "source": f"{len(state['research'])} articles, {len(state['trends'])} trends"}]}

# Parallel fan-out: both start simultaneously
builder = StateGraph(PipelineState)
builder.add_node("researcher",       researcher_node)
builder.add_node("trend_researcher", trend_researcher_node)
builder.add_node("planner",          planner_node)
builder.add_edge(START, "researcher")
builder.add_edge(START, "trend_researcher")    # ← parallel fan-out
builder.add_edge("researcher",       "planner") # ← fan-in: planner waits for BOTH
builder.add_edge("trend_researcher", "planner")
builder.add_edge("planner", END)
graph = builder.compile()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    result = asyncio.run(graph.ainvoke(
        {"topic": "Mumbai real estate 2025", "research": [], "trends": [],
         "content_briefs": [], "creative_drafts": [], "platform_posts": []}
    ))
    print(result["content_briefs"])

productionRef.excerpt:
# Research phase — both run simultaneously
    builder.add_edge(START, "researcher")
    builder.add_edge(START, "trend_researcher")
    builder.add_edge("researcher",       "planner")  # fan-in
    builder.add_edge("trend_researcher", "planner")  # fan-in

    # Creative phase — both run simultaneously
    builder.add_edge("planner",          "social_creative")
    builder.add_edge("planner",          "news_creative")
    builder.add_edge("social_creative",  "internal_retriever")
    builder.add_edge("news_creative",    "internal_retriever")

annotations.text: Both fan in to planner — LangGraph waits for both automatically
Second parallel group: creative phase also fans out and converges

lessons.pyodideCode:
# Demonstrate the operator.add reducer effect
from typing import TypedDict, Annotated
import operator

# Simulate what LangGraph does when merging parallel writes
def merge_without_reducer(updates: list[dict]) -> dict:
    result = {}
    for update in updates:
        result.update(update)   # last write wins!
    return result

def merge_with_add_reducer(base: list, updates: list[dict]) -> list:
    result = list(base)
    for update in updates:
        result = result + update   # operator.add = concatenate
    return result

# Two parallel agents both write creative_drafts
social_output = [{"type": "social", "content": "IPL fever hits Mumbai homes 🏏"}]
news_output   = [{"type": "news",   "content": "Mumbai RE market: Q2 2025 outlook"}]

print("=== Without operator.add (last-write-wins) ===")
result_no_reducer = merge_without_reducer([
    {"creative_drafts": social_output},
    {"creative_drafts": news_output},   # overwrites social!
])
print(f"creative_drafts: {result_no_reducer['creative_drafts']}")
print(f"Count: {len(result_no_reducer['creative_drafts'])} — LOST the social draft!")

print("\n=== With operator.add reducer ===")
result_with_reducer = merge_with_add_reducer(
    [],
    [social_output, news_output]
)
print(f"creative_drafts: {result_with_reducer}")
print(f"Count: {len(result_with_reducer)} — both drafts preserved!")

endQuiz.stem: researcher takes 20s, trend_researcher takes 8s, both run in parallel. Total wall-clock time?
endQuiz.explanation: Parallel execution takes as long as the slowest branch. researcher at 20s is the bottleneck. trend_researcher finishes at 8s and waits. Total = max(20, 8) = 20s.

endQuiz.stem: LangGraph sees two edges pointing TO planner (from researcher and trend_researcher). What does it do?
options: Runs planner twice — once per incoming edge
Waits for ALL upstream nodes to complete before running planner (fan-in)
Runs planner after whichever finishes first
Raises an error — only one incoming edge allowed
endQuiz.explanation: Multiple incoming edges create automatic fan-in. LangGraph waits for all upstream nodes before running the downstream node. No extra code needed.

endQuiz.stem: True or False: asyncio.gather runs Python code on multiple CPU cores simultaneously.
endQuiz.explanation: asyncio is concurrent I/O, not parallel CPU execution. Both agents make network requests that can be 'in-flight' simultaneously — asyncio interleaves the waiting efficiently within one thread.

endQuiz.stem: When should you NOT run two agents in parallel?
options: When both agents make API calls
When agent B needs agent A's output to do its work
When both agents take more than 10 seconds
endQuiz.explanation: Parallel only works when agents are independent. If planner needs researcher's output, it must run AFTER researcher. Data dependencies dictate sequencing.

concept.heading: Why ainvoke for parallel graphs
concept.body:
LLM API calls are I/O-bound — your Python code spends most of its time waiting for the network. `asyncio` lets multiple I/O operations be in-flight simultaneously without threads.

When researcher makes a Tavily API call and trend_researcher makes a Google Trends call, asyncio lets both be pending at the same time. Neither blocks the other.

callouts.title: Switch to ainvoke when adding parallel nodes
callouts.body: graph.invoke() is fine for sequential graphs. The moment you add parallel fan-out (two edges from START), switch to await graph.ainvoke(). LangGraph handles the event loop internally.

callouts.title: For Java/Go engineers: asyncio is NIO, not goroutines
callouts.body:
asyncio uses cooperative multitasking on a single thread — equivalent to Java's NIO with a Selector event loop, not Java threads or Go goroutines. Multiple I/O operations (LLM API calls) are in-flight simultaneously, and the event loop switches between them when one is waiting for a network response.

This means: asyncio.gather([researcher_call, trend_researcher_call]) submits both HTTP requests to the network, then yields the thread to process whichever response arrives first. Total wall time ≈ max(individual times), not sum.

Important constraint: CPU-bound work (parsing large JSON, image processing) won't benefit from asyncio — it blocks the event loop. For CPU-heavy nodes, use asyncio.to_thread() to run in a thread pool.

codeExample.code:
# Sequential graph — invoke() is fine
result = graph.invoke(initial_state)

# Parallel graph — use ainvoke()
import asyncio
result = asyncio.run(graph.ainvoke(initial_state))

# Or inside an async function:
async def run():
    result = await graph.ainvoke(initial_state)

endQuiz.stem: Your graph has two parallel branches (researcher + trend_researcher). You call graph.invoke(). What happens?
options: Both run in parallel as expected
They run sequentially instead of in parallel — no error, just slower
endQuiz.explanation: invoke() is synchronous — it can't truly parallelize I/O. The nodes will still both run but sequentially, eliminating the time benefit. Use ainvoke() to get real concurrency.
